retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the mechanism of action of sulfasalazine. Identify the adverse effects and contraindications of sulfasalazine. Determine appropriate monitoring steps and toxicity of sulfasalazine. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from sulfasalazine therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfasalazine, a potent disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD), helps manage autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and inflammatory bowel disease. This activity discusses sulfasalazine's therapeutic indications, contraindications, and mechanism of action, crucial elements indispensable for the interdisciplinary team involved in patient care. Moreover, the activity focuses on adverse events and other essential facets of sulfasalazine therapy, ensuring healthcare professionals can navigate its complexities to optimize patient outcomes. By exploring off-label applications, pharmacokinetics, monitoring protocols, and clinical toxicology, participants gain an excellent grasp of sulfasalazine's utility, enabling them to tailor treatment strategies for individuals with inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis. With this understanding, healthcare practitioners can fine-tune therapeutic regimens to meet diverse patient needs, enhancing sulfasalazine therapy's efficacy and precision across various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Indications -- Polyarticular Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: Although sulfasalazine has been used for more than 4 decades to treat patients with rheumatoid arthritis, the first multicenter, double-blind, and randomized controlled trial of patients with active juvenile idiopathic arthritis was conducted in 1998. This study has shown that sulfasalazine is effective in treating this condition. Although treatment withdrawal due to adverse events was more frequent in the sulfasalazine-treated group, sulfasalazine was still rated as safe as those events were transient or reversible upon treatment cessation. [6] A follow-up study 10 years later did show significantly better outcome scores, including several active joints, patients' well-being, and duration of the clinical remission of medication in the sulfasalazine group. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: The product labeling does not include dosage adjustments for sulfasalazine; use with caution.

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfasalazine has been widely used to treat inflammatory bowel disease and rheumatoid arthritis. [36] Rheumatology and gastroenterology are the 2 fields that utilize this drug extensively in managing these diseases. Seeing patients in daily practice with these diseases maintained with sulfasalazine is not unusual; collaborating with the primary care team and other specialists is essential for effective management. As mentioned above, the physician must recognize the adverse reactions and contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients: The safety and efficacy of sulfasalazine in patients younger than 2 years old have not been documented. For juvenile idiopathic arthritis patients aged 6 to 16, the recommended dosage is 30 to 50 mg/kg/d, administered in 2 divided doses, with a maximum daily limit of 2 g. Older adults: Older adults with rheumatoid arthritis demonstrated an extended plasma half-life for sulfasalazine, sulfapyridine, and their respective metabolites.

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Indications -- Rheumatoid arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfasalazine is indicated for patients with rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to salicylates or other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Clinical trials have proven that sulfasalazine significantly reduced disease activities, alone or in combination. According to the American College of Rheumatology 2015, sulfasalazine is part of the non-biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs for managing rheumatoid arthritis patients with low to moderate or even high disease activity. [4] A few studies have suggested that genetic polymorphisms, especially genes involved in the folate pathway, affect the efficacy of sulfasalazine in rheumatoid arthritis; this is still an ongoing area of research. Hence, routine genetic testing is not recommended before treatment initiation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Indications -- Rheumatoid arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved sulfasalazine for the treatment of patients with rheumatoid arthritis. It is cheap, easy to administer, and lacks teratogenicity compared to other disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs. There is currently no cure for rheumatoid arthritis. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications and corticosteroids are frequently used to relieve pain and stiffness. However, they do not moderate disease progression. Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs can decrease or delay disease progression. Sulfasalazine has been in use for decades as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug. [1] [2] Newly developed biological disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs include tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-alpha inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, golimumab, certolizumab), anti-CD20 antibodies (rituximab), T-cell targeted therapies (abatacept), interleukin-6 receptor antibody (tocilizumab), interleukin-1 inhibitor (anakinra), and Janus kinase inhibitor (tofacitinib). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Leflunomide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Leflunomide is a medication used in the management and treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. It is in the non-biological DMARDs class of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for leflunomide as a valuable agent in treating and managing rheumatoid arthritis. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors of leflunomide use in a clinical setting, pertinent to members of the interprofessional team in the care of patients with rheumatoid arthritis and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARD) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the difference between traditional and biological DMARDs. Identify the indications for various DMARD agents. Outline the adverse effects and contraindications to biologic DMARD therapy. Describe the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance care delivery for patients when using either traditional or biologic DMARDs. retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Nonbiologic DMARDs
retrieved_docs.content: This category includes methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine (HCQ), azathioprine (AZA), sulfasalazine, leflunomide, and cyclosporine. Methotrexate is the initial drug of choice for patients with RA. The recommended treatment plan recommends an initial dose of 10-15 mg/week of methotrexate with an escalation of 5 mg/month and a target dose of 20-25 mg/week. [98] Subcutaneous administration can be tried in patients who do not respond adequately or are intolerant to oral administration. [99] In patients on methotrexate, 15 mg/week or higher, who still have some disease activity, splitting the dose (10 mg in the morning and 10 mg in the evening every week) increased the bioavailability of methotrexate. [100] Triple therapy, consisting of the combination of methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and sulfasalazine, has been shown to be an effective regimen for RA. [101] [102] However, triple therapy is not well tolerated, and a large percentage of patients do stay on this therapy. [103] [104] [105]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon are both classified as members of the butylpyrazolidine group of anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. As butylpyrazolidine derivatives, these agents share a common chemical scaffold that confers anti‑inflammatory and disease‑modifying properties useful in the treatment of rheumatic disorders. Recognizing fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon as part of this drug class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles within the broader category of butylpyrazolidine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the sulfasalazine compound, indicating that these 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives constitute the active moiety released from sulfasalazine after colonic bacterial metabolism. Sulfasalazine, a combination of sulfapyridine and 5‑aminosalicylic acid, is employed in the treatment of ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, and its therapeutic effect relies on the release of aminosalicylic acid and related agents within the intestine. Recognizing aminosalicylic acid and its analogues as integral components of sulfasalazine helps clinicians understand the drug’s mechanism of action and potential side‑effect profile.

retrieved_docs.content: rather than a therapeutic action. Together, these interrelationships clarify how drug classes, individual agents, chemical families, therapeutic uses, mechanisms of action, contraindications and adverse‑effect profiles are organized, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, anticipating co‑occurring risks and monitoring relevant clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Gold preparations are a class of therapeutic agents that contain gold and are used primarily as disease‑modifying antirheumatic drugs. Auranofin 3 mg oral capsule is an example of a gold preparation, classified as an auranofin formulation administered orally. Aurothiomalate sodium is also a member of the gold preparations class. Both auranofin and aurothiomalate sodium share the anti‑inflammatory properties of gold compounds and are employed in the management of rheumatoid arthritis and related conditions. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same gold preparation category helps clinicians anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles, including potential adverse effects such as dermatitis, nephrotoxicity, and bone‑marrow suppression, and guides appropriate selection of gold‑based therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Fluokortolon and desoximetason are both classified as potent corticosteroids and are included in the category “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations.” As members of this group, they share the pharmacologic characteristics of strong glucocorticoid agents, providing powerful anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and often being incorporated into various combination drug formulations. Recognizing their membership in this potent corticosteroid class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens and anticipating the potency‑related safety considerations associated with high‑strength glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1809
retrieved_docs.content: Gout and other crystal arthropathies are a group of joint disorders characterized by the deposition of crystalline substances in the synovial tissue. Management of these conditions may involve treatment with sulindak, a therapeutic agent that falls under the broader category of Antidotes and Soothing Applications. In addition to its therapeutic role, sulindak’s classification within Antidotes and Soothing Applications highlights its function in alleviating painful and inflammatory joint conditions. Preventive strategies for gout and other crystal arthropathies can include the use of triamcinolonacetonid, a corticosteroid preparation that helps reduce the risk of acute crystal‑induced attacks. When evaluating a patient with suspected gout or other crystal arthropathies, clinicians should consider a differential diagnosis (ddx) that includes inflammatory rheumatic diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي) and infectious or gastrointestinal‑related joint inflammation (التهاب المفاصل المعدي). Recognizing these overlapping clinical entities and employing appropriate therapeutic agents like sulindak and triamcinolonacetonid are essential for accurate diagnosis, effective prevention, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Quinolines, antirheumatic drugs are classified as specific antirheumatic agents, indicating that this subgroup of quinoline compounds is used specifically to treat rheumatic conditions within the broader category of antirheumatic medications.
